question: Soft bodied animals without "backbone" found in the waters of the world have a scientific category called:
explanation: Invertebrates is the broad scientific category for animals that lack a vertebral column — sponges, cnidarians (jellyfish, corals, anemones), molluscs, crustaceans, echinoderms, etc. Cnidarians are a subset of invertebrates; diatoms are single-celled algae.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

question: Creatures capable of inflicting injury will do so only:
explanation: Marine animals do not stalk divers. Bites, stings and strikes happen when the animal feels threatened, cornered or surprised — defensive behaviour. Respect distance, don't touch, don't corner, and 99% of incidents are avoided.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

question: Which plants make up more than 98% of the bulk of the plants produced in the oceans?
explanation: Phytoplankton — microscopic photosynthetic algae drifting in the upper sunlit layer — accounts for ~98% of ocean plant biomass and roughly half of all photosynthesis on Earth. Seaweeds, seagrasses and mangroves are tiny by comparison; corals are animals, not plants.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

question: An incoming tide is called a ____ and an outgoing tide is called an ____.
explanation: Flood tide = water rising / coming in. Ebb tide = water falling / going out. Slack water is the brief calm at the turn between the two — usually the safest time to dive in tidal zones.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

question: The stinging mechanisms of jellyfish are located:
explanation: Nematocysts (stinging capsules) are concentrated on the tentacles, where they're triggered by contact to harpoon prey or deter predators. The mesoglea is the jelly-like body layer; the epidermis and gastrodermis are the outer/inner cell layers but carry far fewer stinging cells.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

question: If a diver is injured by harmful sea life, it is usually because of:
explanation: Most marine-life injuries are diver-caused: ignorance (not knowing what's dangerous), negligence (not watching where hands and fins go), or aggression (chasing, touching, harassing). Education + situational awareness eliminates the vast majority.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

question: A knowledge of underwater terrain helps divers:

options.b: Navigate safely through waves and currents

explanation: Reading underwater topography supports every part of a dive: predicting where marine life will be, anticipating current behaviour around walls and pinnacles, choosing entry/exit points, planning depth profiles and navigation legs.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

question: What is the primary function of seagrass beds in marine ecosystems?

options.a: They prevent all sediment from settling on the seafloor
options.b: They stop seahorses being consumed by predators
options.c: They generate waves and strong currents
options.d: They provide food and habitat for various marine species

explanation: Seagrass beds are nursery habitats and food sources for an enormous range of species — turtles, dugongs, manatees, juvenile fish, invertebrates. They also stabilise sediment and sequester carbon, but their primary ecological role is food + habitat.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

question: Although all corals contain stinging cells, one group is the most troublesome to divers. These are called:
explanation: Fire corals (Millepora — actually hydrozoans, not true corals) deliver a potent burning sting on contact, leaving welts and pain that can last hours. Brain, leather and soft corals are far less aggressive. Standard rule: never touch the reef.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

question: Sponges can be recognized by the presence of ____.
explanation: Sponges (phylum Porifera = 'pore-bearing') filter-feed by drawing water in through countless small pores (ostia) and expelling it through larger openings (oscula). The visible holes are the diagnostic feature.
source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment
